Q1i.scenario: ABC is an established medium-size Indian Health and Care insurer with 25% of its Indian business in the Middle East through JV collaborations. It writes all four types of individual H&C business (IP, CI, PMI, LTCI) in both markets, plus a unique individual deferred unit-linked LTCI product in India. ABC currently has no group products.
Q1i.question: Q1(i) Outline the key risk factors and corresponding rating factors for the Indian and Middle East portfolios for the following products: Individual IP, Individual CI, Individual PMI, Individual LTCI.

Individual IP: Key risk factors: health status (pre-existing conditions), occupation (manual vs sedentary), income level (replacement ratio risk), lifestyle factors (smoking, alcohol), age and gender.
Rating factors: age, gender, occupation class, sum insured/benefit amount, deferred period, definition of disability (own occupation vs any occupation), smoking status.
Individual CI: Key risk factors: medical history and family history (genetic predisposition to covered illnesses), age, gender, smoking status, lifestyle factors.
Rating factors: age, gender, smoking status, sum insured, policy term, number and type of illnesses covered.
Individual PMI: Key risk factors: age, gender, pre-existing conditions, lifestyle factors (BMI, smoking, alcohol), area of residence (access to healthcare), sum insured level.
Rating factors: age, gender, sum insured, network of hospitals, plan type, deductible/co-pay level, zone/geography.
Individual LTCI: Key risk factors: age, health status and functional ability, cognitive ability, family history of dementia/long-term conditions, socio-economic status.
Rating factors: age at entry, benefit amount, benefit escalation, deferred period, policy term (whole life vs fixed term), definition of triggers (ADL-based or cognitive impairment-based).

Q1iii.scenario: ABC is considering expanding its group health business in India. The Head of Business Development proposes a new Group Medical Reimbursement (GMR) product with a unique overseas treatment top-up benefit for employees requiring treatment outside India.
Q1iii.question: Q1(iii) Discuss the key considerations for pricing the overseas treatment top-up benefit.

Data and experience: ABC has no current experience of overseas treatment claims. It will need to source data from: international private medical insurers; reinsurers with experience of expatriate or international health products; published statistics on treatment costs in target countries.
The data will need to be adjusted for the specific profile of the target employee population, the countries covered, and the conditions likely to be treated overseas.
Cost components: The cost of overseas treatment will be significantly higher than Indian treatment costs. Key cost components include: hospital charges, specialist fees, diagnostic costs, accommodation for patient and companion, travel costs, and repatriation costs.
Medical inflation in the target treatment countries needs to be allowed for, as does currency exchange rate risk.
Utilisation and anti-selection: The take-up rate and utilisation of the top-up benefit will be difficult to estimate without prior experience. The benefit is likely to attract higher utilisers — those who already know they may need expensive treatment overseas.
Anti-selection risk is significant. Controls such as pre-authorisation, defined list of covered conditions and countries, and waiting periods can help mitigate this.
Other considerations: Reinsurance will likely be required given the high severity and uncertain frequency of claims. Quota share or excess of loss arrangements should be explored.
The benefit design should specify clearly: which countries are covered; which conditions qualify for overseas treatment; maximum benefit limits; whether the benefit is cashless or reimbursement.
Regulatory approval may be required given the cross-border nature of the benefit.

Q2i.scenario: You are a consulting actuary advising a new standalone health insurance company (SAHI) that is planning to launch a comprehensive health insurance product suite targeting the urban middle class in India.
Q2i.question: Q2(i) Describe the key steps in the pricing process for a new individual PMI product.

Data collection and analysis: Collect relevant data: industry data from IRDAI/GIC; data from reinsurers on Indian morbidity experience; international benchmarks adjusted for Indian conditions.
The data should be analysed to derive: claim incidence rates by age, gender, and benefit type; average claim amounts by diagnosis and procedure; medical cost inflation trends.
Assumptions: Set assumptions for: claim inception rates by age, gender, and plan; average claim amounts; medical inflation; lapses and persistency; expenses (initial, renewal, claims handling); investment returns.
All assumptions should be documented and signed off. Sensitivity analysis should be performed on key assumptions.
Premium calculation: Calculate the risk premium as the expected claims cost per policy year, allowing for: the benefit design (deductibles, co-pays, sub-limits, waiting periods); the underwriting approach and expected selection effect.
Add loadings for: expenses; profit margin; contingency; reinsurance cost (net of recoveries).
Perform a profit test to verify that the product meets the required return on capital at the projected volumes.
Regulatory and competitive review: Ensure premiums comply with IRDAI regulations including any minimum/maximum premium requirements.
Benchmark rates against existing market products to ensure competitiveness.
File the product and premium rates with IRDAI as required under the File & Use / Use & File procedure.

Q2ii.scenario: A new SAHI is planning to launch a comprehensive health insurance product suite. You are the consulting actuary.
Q2ii.question: Q2(ii) Discuss the key challenges in pricing a PMI product for a new insurer with no prior experience data.

Data challenges: The insurer has no own experience data. It will need to rely on: IRDAI industry data; reinsurer data; data from the insurer's distribution partners; published medical research.
Industry data may not be granular enough for the specific product design and target market. Data may not be credible for low-frequency high-severity events.
The data may need significant adjustment to reflect: the insurer's target market demographics; the specific product design; the underwriting approach; the network of providers.
Selection effects: Without historical experience, it is difficult to quantify the anti-selection risk from the underwriting approach (moratorium vs. full medical underwriting).

The insurer needs to allow for the initial selection effect from underwriting, which will make claims lighter in the early years, but this effect wears off over time. This creates uncertainty in pricing multi-year projections.
Medical inflation and trend: Medical inflation in India has been running significantly above consumer price inflation. The appropriate trend assumption is uncertain and the insurer has no own data to validate trends.
Technology changes (new treatments, drugs, diagnostic tests) can significantly affect the cost and frequency of claims in ways that are hard to predict.
Expense and volume uncertainty: With no existing business, the new insurer faces significant uncertainty about: initial and renewal expense levels; the volume of business that will be written (which affects expense per policy); the persistency of the business.
The break-even volume required to cover fixed expenses may be significantly higher than can be achieved in the early years, creating pricing pressure.
